does: This paper identifies skill poisoning as a severe attack vector against LLM agents, where malicious instructions are embedded within legitimate skill definitions. The authors characterize the underlying mechanism as attention hijacking, where poisoned skills cause the model's attention to shift from trusted context to malicious content. They propose RouteGuard, a detector that operates on internal model signals without modifying the frozen backbone, combining response-conditioned attention patterns and hidden-state alignment through reliability-gated late fusion. Evaluated on both real and synthetic skill benchmarks, RouteGuard achieves 0.8834 F1 on critical test slices and recovers 90.51% of description-based attacks that escape lexical defenses, demonstrating that internal-signal monitoring substantially outperforms text-only filtering approaches.
connects: This work extends research on prompt injection attacks to the agent setting, where skills introduce a new attack surface that differs from traditional indirect injection. It builds on prior work examining how LLMs can be misled through carefully crafted input sequences and connects to emerging research on understanding internal mechanisms driving model behavior, particularly through attention analysis. The paper responds to growing concerns about agent safety and the limitations of surface-level defenses like keyword filtering. It leverages insights from mechanistic interpretability work examining how attention patterns reflect model reasoning, applying these insights to the practical detection problem where attacks are hidden in otherwise functional skill definitions.
enables: This work establishes internal-signal detection as a necessary defense primitive for agent systems, opening directions for building more robust safeguards against sophisticated injection attacks. It points toward developing detectors that monitor multiple internal signals beyond attention, potentially combining mechanisms across layers and components. The framework could extend to detecting other forms of subtle model compromise in agent settings, from reward hacking to reward poisoning. Future work might explore how such detectors scale to larger models and more complex skill libraries, whether they generalize across different LLM architectures, and how to integrate them into real-time agent execution pipelines with acceptable computational overhead. The attention-hijacking mechanism also suggests opportunities for developing training-time defenses that make models more resistant to such redirection.
